rationale: The citing paper references Shang, Reed, and Croson (2008) repeatedly to anchor the expected positive identity-congruency effect on donations and to interpret its own Experiment 1b results. It explicitly contrasts its finding (lower donations under heightened gender salience in a threatening cancer context) with Shang et al.'s positive effect in a nonthreatening fundraising domain. The paper also states this as a theoretical contribution, arguing that ego threat is a moderator of identity-congruency donation effects, so the citation plays a clear conceptual role beyond background mention.
relation_description: The citing paper uses the cited study as the prior identity-congruency donation effect, then qualifies that effect by showing it can reverse under identity-threatening health contexts. In doing so, it introduces ego threat/defensive processing as a boundary condition on the earlier donation finding.
